one_line_takeaway: SAFEHARNESS shows that wiring four phase-aligned defense layers (input filtering, tiered verification, capability-based tool constraint, and checkpoint/rollback) together via cross-layer feedback substantially outperforms external guardrail or pipeline-level baselines on Agent-SafetyBench (roughly 19-24 point absolute UBR/ASR reductions across three harnesses and two DeepSeek-V3.2 modes) while mostly preserving task completion, but the gains were measured on a 200-task simulated-tool subset with LLM-judge scoring, not a real deployed backend.
key_finding: Aggregated over all conditions (clean + five attack types), SAFEHARNESS achieves the lowest UBR and ASR of any configuration on every harness x model combination in Table 5. Under DeepSeek-Chat it reduces UBR relative to Unprotected by 22.4 points on ReAct (51.18%->28.74%), 18.7 points on Multi-Agent (44.99%->26.31%), and 22.8 points on Self-Evolving (61.98%->39.18%), with corresponding ASR reductions of 22.8, 19.2, and 22.9 points. Under DeepSeek-Reasoner, which starts from a higher unprotected UBR (e.g. 57.32% on ReAct), SAFEHARNESS cuts UBR by 22.7-23.7 points and ASR by 23.9-24.7 points across all three harnesses. The paper's stated headline figures (abstract) are an average ~38% UBR reduction and ~42% ASR reduction versus the unprotected baseline. Utility is largely preserved: TCR stays within about 1-2 points of Unprotected on ReAct and Self-Evolving; on Multi-Agent TCR drops from 86.50% to 77.92% while UA rises from 48.10% to 59.40%, which the authors interpret as blocking unsafe completions rather than losing capability. UA under attack improves substantially, e.g. from 45.60% to 67.50% on Chat-ReAct. Blk (intercepted actions) scales with integration depth: Guardrail and LlamaFirewall intercept only 88 and 168 actions on Chat-ReAct, versus 1,152 for SAFEHARNESS on the same cell (1,477 on Self-Evolving). Per-attack breakdown (Section 4.3) shows composite (A5) and memory-injection (A4) attacks remain the hardest even under SAFEHARNESS, especially on Self-Evolving where persistent memory amplifies the injection surface (A4 UBR: 55.96% Chat, 56.48% Reasoner), while context poisoning (A1) and indirect injection (A2) see the largest relative reductions versus the Guardrail/LlamaFirewall baselines. Multi-Agent achieves the lowest SAFEHARNESS ASR (20.2% Chat, 28.7% Reasoner) but the highest Blk and sharpest TCR drop, illustrating a deployment-tunable false-negative/false-positive trade-off from dual-agent verification overhead.
core_contribution: SAFEHARNESS is a security architecture that embeds four defense layers directly into the four phases of the LLM agent execution lifecycle (input processing, decision making, action execution, state update), rather than bolting external guardrails onto the harness boundary. The layers are: L1 INFORM (structural sanitization + regex pattern detection + LLM-based semantic filtering + provenance tagging of every content chunk entering the context, applied to both user input and tool output); L2 VERIFY (a three-tier escalating verification cascade: deterministic rule-based risk scoring, an LLM contextual judge, and LLM-approximated causal diagnostics that attribute an ambiguous tool call to legitimate intent vs. adversarial injection); L3 CONSTRAIN (least-privilege enforcement via five risk tiers -- read_only, write, execute, network, destructive -- time/invocation-limited signed capability tokens, and HMAC-SHA256 integrity verification of tool descriptions in the registry); and L4 CORRECT (periodic state checkpointing including the memory store, attack-triggered rollback, and a five-level adaptive degradation/recovery mechanism that tightens or relaxes the L3 tier ceiling based on recent safe/unsafe action history). The paper's actual novelty, per its own framing, is not any single layer's technique (each individual mechanism -- sanitization, judge models, capability tokens, rollback -- draws on prior art) but the cross-layer feedback wiring that couples the four layers: L1->L2 escalation on detected injection or untrusted provenance, L2->L4 rollback/degradation on confirmed or Tier-2 unsafe verdicts, L4->L3 privilege tightening tied to degradation level, and L4->L2 de-escalation on recovery, plus an orthogonal entropy monitor that tracks a windowed constraint-violation rate (not Shannon entropy) and escalates verification tier when it exceeds a threshold (default w=20, theta=0.3). The paper explicitly frames this as the first architecture to integrate defense mechanisms across all four lifecycle phases with such inter-layer feedback, as opposed to prior component-level or single-checkpoint defenses.
threat_model: Formalized as an agent tuple (M, T, E, H) -- model, tools, execution environment, harness. The adversary can compromise external channels but cannot modify model weights or harness code. Concretely the adversary may: (i) inject adversarial instructions into user inputs/task descriptions, (ii) embed malicious payloads in tool outputs (e.g., poisoned web content or RAG retrievals), (iii) modify tool descriptions in the registry, and (iv) inject fabricated entries into conversation history. The paper defines six threat vectors (Table 1): T1 Direct Injection, T2 Indirect Injection, T3 Tool Abuse, T4 Tool Tampering, T5 Memory Corruption, T6 Privilege Escalation, each mapped to a target lifecycle phase and to primary/secondary defense layers. These are experimentally instantiated as five attack types (A1 Context Poison targeting L1/T1 with 8 payload variants, A2 Indirect Inject targeting L1/T2 simulating RAG poisoning, A3 Tool Tamper targeting L3/T3+T4+T6 via description modification and parameter escalation, A4 Memory Inject targeting the memory layer/T5 via fabricated conversation history, and A5 Composite combining A1+A2+A3+A4 simultaneously), plus a clean no-attack control.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) the tool execution environment is simulated, not deployed against real backends, so production transfer is unvalidated; (2) both L2 verification and final evaluation rely on LLM-as-Judge, introducing potential judge bias, only partially mitigated by keeping agent and judge models distinct and cross-validating with deterministic rule checks; (3) the multi-tier L2 cascade adds LLM inference cost for actions escalating past Tier 1, though most safe actions resolve at the cheap Tier-1 rule engine; (4) attack implementations, while grounded in documented threat taxonomies (Greshake et al. 2023 indirect prompt injection, OWASP Top 10 for LLM apps), cover only a subset of the adversarial landscape -- adaptive adversaries that specifically target SAFEHARNESS's own detection patterns are explicitly left as an open problem. The paper also positions Spider-Sense (selective/intrinsic risk-triggered activation), AgentDoG (trajectory-level root-cause diagnostics), and LlamaFirewall (pipeline-level pattern+audit) as complementary/orthogonal rather than subsumed by SAFEHARNESS, i.e. it does not claim to solve efficiency-selective activation or explicit trajectory-level diagnosis.

datasets_used: Agent-SafetyBench (Zhang et al., 2024a): 2,000 safety-critical tasks, 349 interactive environments, 8 risk categories; 735 fulfillable / 1,265 refusal-required tasks; 111-task multi-turn subset; main experiments use first 200 tasks in default order
quantitative_results: Abstract headline: ~38% average UBR reduction and ~42% average ASR reduction vs. unprotected baseline
DeepSeek-Chat ReAct: UBR 51.18% (Unprotected) -> 28.74% (SAFEHARNESS); ASR 49.20% -> 26.40%; TCR 95.25% -> 94.25%; UA 45.60% -> 67.50%; Blk 0 -> 1152
A4 (memory injection) residual UBR under SAFEHARNESS on Self-Evolving: 55.96% (Chat), 56.48% (Reasoner) -- the hardest attack condition reported
Guardrail baseline interceptions (Blk) on Chat-ReAct: 88; LlamaFirewall: 168, vs. SAFEHARNESS: 1152 on the same cell
Entropy monitor default parameters: sliding window w=20 actions, escalation threshold theta=0.3 windowed violation rate

L3 five risk tiers: read_only, write, execute, network, destructive; L4 five degradation levels (0=all tiers permitted through 4=read_only only); adaptive recovery after a configurable window (e.g. 5 consecutive safe steps)
baselines_compared: System-Prompt (safety policy embedded in the model prompt, relies on LLM compliance)
Guardrail (external I/O filter modeled on NeMo Guardrails / Llama Guard, no harness-state visibility)
LlamaFirewall (reimplementation of PromptGuard-style pattern detection + AlignmentCheck-style LLM reasoning audit, per Chennabasappa et al. 2025; no privilege enforcement or rollback)
